symbols: bound arms raised above — total vulnerability and surrender in martyrdom
tree trunk — the post of execution echoing the wood of the cross
bare torso — stripped of worldly identity, laid bare before God
dark foliage behind — the shadow of death through which the martyr passes
arrow wound — the instrument of martyrdom piercing the faithful witness
description: A half-length figure of a young man with dark curly hair, his bare torso exposed, arms bound above his head to a pale tree trunk. He wears only a white loincloth. His body shows signs of an arrow wound. He looks outward with a calm, almost defiant expression. The background is dark with foliage and sky, painted in loose, impressionistic brushstrokes. The warm flesh tones contrast with the cool, dark surroundings.
theological_description: Corot's Saint Sebastian presents the early Christian martyr with a painterly intimacy that emphasizes his humanity rather than the spectacular nature of his execution. Sebastian, a Roman soldier and secret Christian under Emperor Diocletian, was sentenced to death by arrows for his faith. While not a biblical figure, his martyrdom directly embodies New Testament teachings about bearing witness unto death. Jesus warned his followers: 'If the world hates you, know that it has hated me before it hated you... If they persecuted me, they will also persecute you' (John 15:18, 20). Sebastian's calm expression reflects Paul's confidence: 'For I am sure that neither death nor life, nor angels nor rulers, nor things present nor things to come, nor powers, nor height nor depth, nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord' (Romans 8:38-39). The binding to the tree evokes Christ's own binding before his crucifixion and Peter's description: 'He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree' (1 Peter 2:24). Sebastian's endurance in suffering became a model for Christian courage under persecution.

scripture_refs.reason: Jesus warning of the world's hatred and persecution — the context for Sebastian's martyrdom
Nothing able to separate from God's love — Sebastian's calm amid suffering reflects this confidence
Christ bearing sins on the tree — Sebastian bound to a tree in imitation of his Lord's passion
Be faithful unto death and receive the crown of life — the call Sebastian answered
